title: Greetings & Goodbyes
shortSummary: Greet people, introduce yourself, and say goodbye in Hindi, the foundation of every conversation.
culturalNote: The all-purpose Hindi greeting is namaste (नमस्ते), said with palms pressed together at the chest and a slight bow. It works morning, noon, and night, with strangers and elders alike, and it doubles as a goodbye. For Hindus, namaskar (नमस्कार) is a slightly more respectful variant; Muslims often greet with adaab or assalam alaikum, and Sikhs with sat sri akal, so listen for community cues. The biggest social choice is the word for 'you': aap (आप) is polite and respectful, tum (तुम) is friendly and informal, and tu (तू) is intimate or, with the wrong person, rude. As a learner you should default to aap with everyone until you are clearly invited to be casual. Touching an elder's feet (पैर छूना) is a deep mark of respect at family gatherings and festivals.

keyPhrases.english: How are you? (to a man, polite)
How are you? (to a woman, polite)

grammar.rule: Aap vs tum vs tu (respect levels)
grammar.explanation: Hindi has three words for 'you'. Aap (आप) is polite and takes plural verb forms; tum (तुम) is informal and friendly; tu (तू) is intimate or for children, and can be rude with adults. As a learner, use aap with everyone you do not know well.
grammar.commonMistake: Using tu with an elder or stranger, which sounds disrespectful.

grammar.explanation: Adjectives and some question words change for the gender of the person spoken to. Kaise (कैसे) is used for a man and kaisi (कैसी) for a woman, even though both mean 'how'.
grammar.commonMistake: Using kaise for a woman; the ending should change to kaisi.

dialogue.setup: Riya meets her new colleague Mr. Sharma at the office in the morning.

lines.speaker: Mr. Sharma
lines.native: नमस्ते! मैं ठीक हूँ, धन्यवाद। और आप?
lines.transliteration: namaste! main theek hoon, dhanyavaad. aur aap?
lines.english: Hello! I'm fine, thank you. And you?

lines.speaker: Riya
lines.native: मैं भी ठीक हूँ। मेरा नाम रिया है।
lines.transliteration: main bhi theek hoon. mera naam Riya hai.
lines.english: I'm fine too. My name is Riya.

lines.speaker: Mr. Sharma
lines.native: आपसे मिलकर खुशी हुई। मैं शर्मा हूँ।
lines.transliteration: aapse milkar khushi hui. main Sharma hoon.
lines.english: Nice to meet you. I am Sharma.

lines.speaker: Riya
lines.native: मुझे भी खुशी हुई। टीम में आपका स्वागत है।
lines.transliteration: mujhe bhi khushi hui. team mein aapka swaagat hai.
lines.english: I'm pleased too. Welcome to the team.

lines.speaker: Mr. Sharma
lines.english: Thank you very much. See you again.

quiz.question: How do you say 'Hello' in Hindi (the all-purpose polite greeting)?
quiz.explanation: Namaste works at any time of day and also means goodbye.

quiz.question: Which word for 'you' is the polite, respectful one?
quiz.explanation: Aap is polite and takes plural verb forms; learners should default to it.

quiz.question: आप _____ हैं? (How are you, asking a woman)
quiz.explanation: Kaisi is the feminine form; kaise is used for a man.

quiz.question: What does 'धन्यवाद (dhanyavaad)' mean?
quiz.explanation: Dhanyavaad is the standard polite 'thank you'.

quiz.question: Arrange: हूँ / मैं / ठीक (I am fine)
quiz.explanation: Hindi word order is Subject-Object-Verb, so the verb hoon comes last.
